cover.title: 7 dark psychology tricks people use on you:
cover.subtitle: (without you ever noticing)

slides.heading: 1. Mirroring you on purpose.
slides.body:
They copy your tone,
your posture, your words.
It feels like chemistry.
It's actually a tactic to
lower your defenses.

slides.heading: 2. The forced choice.
slides.body:
"Do you want to meet Friday or Saturday?"
They skip asking IF you want to meet.
You pick a day, thinking you had control.
You never did.

slides.heading: 3. Strategic silence.
slides.body:
After a hard question,
they go quiet.
Most people rush to fill the gap
and reveal more than they meant to.
Silence is leverage.

slides.heading: 4. The small favor first.
slides.body:
They ask for something tiny.
You say yes.
Now asking for something bigger
feels consistent — not pushy.
It's called the foot-in-the-door.

slides.heading: 5. Flipping blame mid-sentence.
slides.body:
You raise an issue.
Within two sentences
you're the one apologizing.
That's not a conversation.
That's a redirect.

slides.heading: 6. Love-bombing on day one.
slides.body:
Compliments. Attention. Intensity.
Fast.
It isn't love — it's a deposit.
They'll withdraw it later
when they want something.

slides.heading: 7. Rewriting what just happened.
slides.body:
You remember the argument one way.
They calmly insist it went another.
Do this enough times
and the target stops trusting
their own memory.

slides.heading: The shift.
slides.body:
You can't stop people
from running these plays.
You can stop being
the easiest target in the room.
Awareness is the whole game.

slides.prompt: Which trick has been used on you?
slides.follow: Follow **@millionairemovex** for the things no one warns you about.
slides.share: Save this — and send it to someone who keeps falling for it.
